attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with acute left-sided abdominal pain and nausea. She has never experienced this type of abdominal pain before. Her last menstrual period was two weeks ago. She is sexually active and takes the progesterone only pill for contraception. There are no signs of dysuria, polyuria or abnormal vaginal discharge.

On examination, her pulse rate is 85/min, blood pressure is 130/75mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, and temperature 36.5°C. There is tenderness in the left iliac fossa with no guarding or rigidity. Bimanual examination reveals left adnexal tenderness. Speculum examination is unremarkable.

Urinary beta HCG is negative, and a serum beta HCG is also negative. First pass urine NAAT is negative for chlamydia and gonorrhoea. A transvaginal ultrasound shows several small ovarian cysts bilaterally with no signs of ovarian torsion. The largest ovarian cyst is 25mm in diameter. There is minimal free fluid within the Pouch of Douglas.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Seven day course of oral doxycycline
attributes.explanation:
There are a number of potential causes for this patient's acute abdominal pain. She is likely suffering from an ovarian cyst accident. This is a clinical diagnosis based on focal lower abdominal pain, timing in relation to the menstrual cycle (mid-cycle is common) and combined with a normal pelvic ultrasound and negative b-hCG. Other differentials include ovarian torsion, ectopic pregnancy, pelvic inflammatory disease and appendicitis.

Ideally, women with such a diagnosis and who are stable and not requiring intravenous analgesia should be discharged home with the expectation of the pain resolving in the next 2-4 days. It is important to have a plan for follow-up with the GP if there is a cyst present or the pain has not resolved. Oral analgesia should be prescribed in accordance to the patients' pain severity.

These patients should be considered for repeat ultrasound in 6-8 weeks as most cysts <5cm in pre-menopausal women are are functional (either follicular or corpus luteum) and will resolve spontaneously within two to three menstrual cycles. Per RCOG guidelines, asymptomatic simple cysts 30-50 mm do not require follow-up, cysts 50-70 mm require ultrasound follow-up, and cysts more than 70 mm should be considered for either further imaging (MRI) or surgical intervention. However, as this patient is symptomatic, follow-up imaging is warranted regardless of cyst size.

There are a few sinister complications of ruptured ovarian cysts that clinicians should be aware of including infection and bleeding. Patients who are haemodynamically unstable should be admitted for observation, pain management and fluid resuscitation.

Diagnostic laparoscopy is not required for stable patients with suspected ovarian cyst rupture. Diagnostic laparoscopy is indicated for patients with suspected appendicitis, ovarian torsion or ectopic pregnancy which are less likely in this case due to the ultrasound findings.

Observation in hospital may be considered for patients who are unstable or require more intensive pain management, but this patient is stable and can be managed as an outpatient.

Pelvic CT scan should be avoided in women of reproductive age. Ultrasound is the investigation of choice when investigating for adnexal pathology and has low risks to the patient.

Seven day course of oral doxycycline is the appropriate management for patients with suspected chlamydia infection which is less likely in this case due to the negative FPU NAAT results.
learningPoints: Ovarian cyst rupture presents with acute focal abdominal pain often in the middle of the menstrual cycle.
Management of a ruptured ovarian cyst in a stable patient typically involves outpatient treatment with oral analgesia.
Ultrasound is the preferred imaging modality for evaluating adnexal pathology in women of reproductive age.
references: Bottomley C, Bourne T. Diagnosis and management of ovarian cyst accidents. Best Pract Res Clin Obstet Gynaecol. 2009 Oct;23(5):711-24. doi: 10.1016/j.bpobgyn.2009.02.001. Epub 2009 Mar 18. PMID: 19299205. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19299205/

Mobeen S, Apostol R. Ovarian Cyst. [Updated 2023 Jun 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560541/
Brott NR, Le JK. Mittelschmerz. [Updated 2023 May 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK549822/

Sharp HT. Evaluation and management of ruptured ovarian cyst. UpToDate. UpToDate. 2019. [Internet]. Accessed on Jan 13 2025. Available from: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/ovarian-and-fallopian-tube-torsion
